Order of kwargs is a tricky one, though. From an API perspective, if people called `equalize_adapthist(im, 8, 8, 0.001, 128)` previously you are correct - the only way not to break that API is dumping the new kwarg at the end. Another good reason to name your kwargs ;) Still, Python allows this use mode so someone may be using it.

Unfortunately, as the kernel size is crucial to the adaptive nature of this algorithm, control over this _should_ immediately follow the primary argument. So we either hold fast to our API breakage policy and ship a poor API as a result, or we break it.

In this specific case, I'd recommend breaking the API in the name of having a good future API path. We could make the change slightly less disruptive by ordering the arguments `image, ntiles_x, ntiles_y, kernel_size, ...` but I don't think having `kernel_size` at the end is a great move. And, while it is technically a "breakage" of the API, it will affect only a subset of users.

memory sharing isn't well documented in mxnet. The link only is part of how memory sharing is implemented. Essentially this is for all ndarrays that are in python space. Additionally there is logic for sharing ndarrays allocated in C++ land between the C++ executor objects. The executor of the default bucket key is where ndarrays for all other executors come from. The logic is sort of like this pseudo code:
```
unused_ndarrays = default_executor.ndarrays
for ndarray_size in ndarrays_needed_for_executor:
  ndarray = unused_ndarrays.pop_smallest_that_fits(ndarray_size)
  if ndarray == None:
     ndarray = new NDarray()

```
This is happening somewhere here:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/executor/graph_executor.cc#L917 


Bottom line is that the ndarrays of the executor for the default bucket key need to be large enough to fit ndarrays of all other executors. If so no additional memory is required.

I'd still advocate for making the elements of `bucket_batch_sizes ` a `namedtuple("bucket_batch_size", ["batch_size", "word_per_batch"])`, to make `[-1][0]` a little clearer by using `[-1].batch_size` instead.

> You assumption is essentially that collectors that have problems describing themselves will also be those that are expensive to collect().

In the case of `del_txt_record`: it doesn't matter if we report cleanup before the change has been made

In the case of `_find_managed_zone_id`: it's a read operation, so there's no status.

We re-execute the request to get the updated `status` value from the server.
